title: Cat Ear Canal Swollen Shut: Causes, Urgency, and Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat's ear canal swollen shut? Learn about common causes, urgency levels, and why prompt veterinary care is essential for relief and hearing.
content_html:
TL;DR: A cat’s ear canal swollen shut is usually caused by chronic inflammation and requires veterinary attention within 24 to 48 hours to manage pain and prevent permanent ear damage.

What causes my cat's ear canal to swell shut?

When a cat's ear canal is swollen shut, it is typically a condition called chronic otitis externa or proliferative tissue change. This occurs when the skin lining the ear canal becomes so inflamed and thickened that the opening narrows or completely closes. Common causes include long-term ear infections, severe allergies, ear mites, or inflammatory polyps. Because the canal is closed, wax and discharge become trapped, often leading to a secondary, deep-seated infection.

Is a swollen ear canal in my cat an emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Medium.
  • While rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it is a source of significant pain and discomfort.
  • If left untreated, the infection can migrate to the middle or inner ear, potentially causing permanent hearing loss, balance issues, or a ruptured eardrum.
  • You should aim to see a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours to begin professional treatment and pain management.

How does sending a photo of my cat's ear help with the triage process?

  • A clear, well-lit photo allows the veterinary team to see the severity of the swelling and the color of any visible discharge.
  • A photo helps identify whether there are any growths or foreign bodies near the opening.
  • This visual information helps the clinic determine how quickly your cat needs to be squeezed into the schedule.
  • It allows the team to prepare the necessary diagnostic tools before you even arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Severe or chronic otitis externa can cause significant swelling of the ear canal. Treatment hinges on identifying and addressing the primary cause, removing exudate through irrigation, and managing concurrent otitis media if present. Glucocorticoids such as prednisolone or triamcinolone are often beneficial to reduce swelling, even with concurrent antimicrobial use. Pain management should be included in the initial treatment, with tramadol considered for the first 5-7 days. In severe cases unresponsive to medical management, total ear canal ablation may be necessary, particularly if multi-drug resistant bacteria are present or if neoplasia is suspected.

Chapter: Otolaryngology, Oncology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 534)

faqs.question: Can I use over-the-counter drops to open the ear canal?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid putting any medications or cleaners into a swollen ear canal unless directed by a vet. If the eardrum is ruptured behind the swelling, certain medications can be toxic to the inner ear and cause permanent damage.

faqs.question: What will the veterinarian do for a swollen ear?
faqs.answer: The vet will likely perform a physical exam, take a swab for cytology to identify the infection type, and may prescribe anti-inflammatory steroids to reduce the swelling alongside antibiotics or antifungals.

faqs.question: Does this condition require surgery?
faqs.answer: In some chronic cases where the ear canal has become permanently thickened and 'mineralized,' surgery (such as a TECA) may be required. However, many cases can be managed with aggressive medical therapy if caught early enough.
